sentences.text: Yes, I understand.
I understand.
Yes.
The First World War.
I have very few impressions from then, except I knew my dad was there.
He came home a couple of times.
Was in hospital, he was wounded.
And we struggled in our store, which was run by Mother, very efficiently.
And I really had very-- I suffered very little.
I remember one incident when mother got a hold of half a kilo of lump sugar, which was a rarity.
And she gave it to me.
And I-- I rationed it out.
I had one lump every day after my mid-- my mid-day meal.
So it lasted a long time.
But that is just typical, because we had no sweets.
In-- In 1918, I remember when the masses of people flocked into the inner city when the Republic was declared, when it became known that Kaiser Francis-- Kaiser Karl had abdicated.
And for a while, everything was all right.
I didn't have more experiences with my religion.
But I also remember-- I have to backtrack-- I remember the funeral procession of Kaiser Francis Joseph, who passed the main artery in Vienna, Mariahilfer Strasse.

And we were invited to people who had a window facing out, and we saw the cortege going from Schonbrunn, the summer palace, where the kaiser died, to the inner city, to the [GERMAN], where he were-- was then laid out and seen for display.
That's one of the impressions I had.
At that time, I was not affected, not interested, until about, I would say, until I was about 11 years old.
At that time, my mother, who was a very good piano player, took me to my first concert, in [INAUDIBLE].
That is the biggest and best-known venue in Vienna.
Supposedly the best acoustics of that-- of that time, and maybe even now.
And there was a piano soloist, and Mother joked, after it was over, and she said, well, how do you compare my playing to him?
Do you like me playing as well as that one?
That's one of the impressions I had at that time.
Another thing I remember [? to ?] the High Holidays.
Grandmother on my dad's side had bought, for years and years, two tickets for the High Holidays for the synagogue in her district, which was the quasi-Jewish ghetto in Vienna, Leopoldstadt.
It's known as that.
Most of the Polish refugees, from 1914, '15, when they came to Vienna because Poland was battle area, they settled in that district.
And again, there was also the main Vienna temple, the grand-- the Grand Temple of Vienna was in that district.
And grandmother had bought two tickets for-- as a member.
And we two boys, my older brother and I, when we were there during the holidays, we shared that one extra seat, which was in the men's section.
Grandmother had the other seat in the ladies section, of course.
Because in the Orthodox temple, men and women do not sit together.
In the conservative and reform, of course, they shared the-- the-- the seating facilitates, the pews.
Music, yes.
Mother took me to that concert, and she was very musical.
She played the piano wherever she had a chance.
We did not own a piano.
That came a few years later.
And when I was about 14, I got a violin from a relative.
I don't know what their names it was anymore.
And I started taking violin lessons from a neighbor, who was not a violin teacher, but he played.
And after three or four months, he told my parents, well, I've taught him everything I know.
Maybe you should get him a teacher.
I got a teacher who I hated, who was not-- It was very unpleasant situation and I dropped the violin.
But at the same time, when I was in school, we had singing instructions.
Twice a week for one hour was singing instructions.
And I was very fortunate.
Our singing teacher was a man who became, later on, the conductor of the opera, the Vienna Opera Choir and of the Philharmonic Choir in Vienna.
His name was Professor Ferdinand Grossman, very well-known.
And he really inspired us boys in school.
He really did some nice work.
We performed on parents' days and so on.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just started singing, and he let me sing until my voice changed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, Dad bought a piano so mother could play whenever she wanted.
And I started taking piano lessons, which I hated and dropped within a year, and I went into politics.
But I always spent a lot of my allowance on concert tickets and never dropped music.
I went to concerts wherever I could, so I became one of those act-- not active, but passive musicians in Vienna.
I went mainly to concerts.
I was not too-- too happy about opera, but concerts, choir music, that was my-- my liking to this day.
They were playing, but from the time I was 11, I had joined the Boy Scouts in Vienna.
And our Sundays were usually spent in the Vienna woods on hikes, later on, on skiing.
And I did not frequent the Stadtpark or similar places for concerts.
But during the summer, in Vienna Book Garten, they had regular concerts, and I went there many times, again restricted by availability of cold cash.
Because for those things, you had to pay.
Yes.
Yes.
I was bar mitzvah, very peculiar.
I became 13 and Grandmother, who was the Orthodox lady, and-- wanted me to become bar mitzvah'd.
Now, normally, you went to Hebrew school a couple of years before.
In my case, my dad took me to the local rabbi-- we knew where he lived.
He lived in a-- same street as we were.
And Dad took me there one afternoon and said, I would like my son to become bar mitzvah.
His birthday is then and then.
And the rabbi checked his calendar and said, well, the 6th of September, we have one opening.
The [NON-ENGLISH] is already given away for that day, but he can come up there and join the [NON-ENGLISH] boy and say the [NON-ENGLISH].
More, he can do-- can't do anyway.
So he let me recite the [NON-ENGLISH], which I had memorized at home.
The rabbi said, OK.

At that-- on that day, it was a Saturday, of course, you come to the synagogue, and you'll join the [NON-ENGLISH] boy at the [? bima, ?] and you will say the [NON-ENGLISH], and that will be your bar mitzvah.
In the afternoon, I had a few family members at home for coffee and cake.
And that was the reception.
And in late afternoon, then we had my classmates.
That was-- we were all in the-- about the same age.
And every other Saturday or some, there was another party for classmates.
We didn't care for the religious aspect of it.
For us, it was the party.
We exchanged little gifts.
I got my first fountain pen, which was standard present for the bar mitzvah boy.
The fountain pen.
We got the first fountain pen from our bar mitzvah.
Of course it was liquid, with liquid ink we had to fill, and the regular writing pen, not what we have now, a ballpoint.
That wasn't my bar mitzvah.
Pesach I didn't know until much later in life.
And just as I said, the high holidays we spent mostly with our grandmother at the grand synagogue in the center in Leopoldstadt.
In other words, there was no Jewish life in my life.
I was much more interested and active in political life, because by the time I was 13, 14, I already had gone into the left wing, into the Social Democratic Youth Movement.
My parents were members of the Social Democratic Party, and so the influence was given.
I was in the Social Democratic Gymnastic Club and I played-- I was active in sports, track.
I played ball with them.
Completely left-oriented and [INAUDIBLE] was 15 joined-- when I was 15 or 16, I joined the Academic Legion of the Republikanischer Schutzbund where I have the picture.
I was with them until, well, 1925 or 1926.
Active, we exercised, we marched, we got shooting instructions.
We went out to protect [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Meetings of the socialist students, middle school, high school students.
We wore a uniform.
I was active in [? political ?] life.
Zionist?
When I was 11, I joined for a very few months, because it was closest to home, the Maccabi [INAUDIBLE] in our district.
But that was only for a very few months.
I left there soon and went to [NON-ENGLISH], the Social Democratic Gymnastic Club.
That's where I spent most of my time.
Well, we followed the newspapers.
We knew-- I would like to backtrack and just mention the one thing.
In 1921, we had, in Vienna, hunger riots in the center of the city.
All of a sudden, they were rioting, looting food stores.
And when we got word of it, we closed our store, rolled the shutters down, and that afternoon we sent the women workers home.
And that afternoon, we just-- we were closed up.
But that was, as far as I remember, just one afternoon, hunger riots in Vienna, 1921.
No, they were not.
We read the newspapers.
But we didn't know too much about it.
Those things really started affecting us in 1927.
When they had the process in-- the politically processing Vienna, when two right-wingers got acquitted for murder which had occurred early in spring.
In the easternmost province, in Burgenland, there was a demonstration. two of the-- the labor people who had been there had been assassinated.

And they were brought to court in Vienna in '27, July 15, 1927, they were acquitted and the organized labor in Vienna marched from the outlying districts from the various factories where there was, of course, always groups organized, union groups, and marched into the city.
And it ended up with Justizpalast [? in ?] [? those ?] [? days ?] the main Superior Court building in Vienna, Justizpalast.
I was-- I happened to be, at that time already, a member of the Academic [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [?
Schutzbund. ?] And that morning, I was on a delivery trip for my dad's company to a company in the city.
And we passed over the square where the Justizpalast was standing.
And we saw already the masses of union groups there and the first fist fights and arguments with the police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The police had already brought out the mounted force and we kept running, because when they came and eventually, the Justizpalast was set on fire.
I was in a side street with my mother by the way, and we finally made our way home.
When we got home, we-- I got into uniform, footprint uniform, and reported to our local mobilization station.
And I spent the next several days in uniform, occasionally on patrol duty in one of the outer districts.
But that was political, non-religious.
One other thing I remember about religion-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when you did not pass your exam in religion, and our rabbi was very strict.
He tried to teach us Hebrew, but he was a poor teacher.
He was an excellent rabbi, but he was a very poor teacher.
And when I again had not passed an exam, he told me he's going to flunk me for that year.
And if you flunked religion, it was the only subject in school, you had to repeat the year.
Whereas any other, you were given the chance of making up after the vacation, a make up exam, and you passed on to the next grade.
In religion, it didn't exist.
And I told him, point frank, if you flunk me, I am over 14, which was the cutoff level, I'm going down to the nearest magistrate, and I renounce my religion.
That's the way I felt about religion at that time.
He, of course, gave me a passing grade.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
Both my brother and I, we were circumcised.
And I'm sure all the other boys in the family.
My son is circumcised.
I don't know about the others.
And we were always maintaining our Jewish origin, but we were non-religious.
We were ethnic Jews, as I am today.
My mother and her family many times spoke Czech.
They came from-- from Bohemia.
And at the very early day, until about three, we had Bohemian maids.
It was standard in Vienna.
Vienna was always very great with Bohemian, Czech cooks and maids and shoemakers and tailors.
They were typical Czech professions.
And so Czech was very common in Vienna.
Until I was three, I only spoke Czech.
But I forgot it, of course.
And in my family, we only spoke German.

There was no Yiddish, no Jewish, except when my mother got together with her mother and her sisters still like speak Czech, especially if they didn't want the boys to know what they were talking about.
Well, I did not graduate from-- well, I went to [INAUDIBLE].
We had at that time two branches of education in Vienna.
The one was the [INAUDIBLE], which taught mainly French and English, and which opened your way into engineer, to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH].

And in the applied physics, applied chemistry and so on, whereas the other branch was the gymnasium, where they taught Latin and then the Greek, which was the road to medicine, to law, to theology, and theoretical physics and chemistry.
It was the division.
A few-- a few years later, they were joined and it became the [?
Royale ?] Gymnasium.
The two were joined, and you had your choice.
And of course, if you wanted to switch, you had to make up language courses and so on.
But there were-- there was one middle of the road, the technical preparatory school.
I would say almost like a junior college, like we have it here.
For that, I didn't need-- I didn't have to graduate from gymnasium or [INAUDIBLE].
It took four or five years, and then went to that school for five years.
That was called, that's the school where I went, [INAUDIBLE].
I graduated from there in electrotechnique.
And at that time, we did not get the engineer's title.
That was only arranged after the World War II, that the graduates of this particular school and parallel schools on several places in Vienna were allowed to carry the engineer's title.
Today, when I go to Vienna, I am Herr Engineer Snyder.
Yeah.
Well, by that time I had left the active party life.
I was involved in our store.
We had-- we depended on general movement.
We were not carrying the red flag anymore.
Also in 1933, I believe was the year when Chancellor Dollfuss got assassinated in Vienna.
And one of the assassins, I remember the name very well, was the son of the custodian of our apartment building.
That's how I knew the man's name.
And it struck me.
I had met him occasionally when they were here.
And he got hanged in Vienna for the assassination.
But I remember the name.
Hitler?
Hitler was '38 already?
No, no, no, no, no, no, wait--
Oh, in Germany.
That-- that we found out later on.
I think I had found that out already when I was in the States.
And when I visited Vienna later on in the '80s, I made it my business to go down and look at the house.
There is not even a plaque in front.
There might have been at one time.
But it wasn't there anymore.
But we learned through that, we had a radio, which I had manufactured myself, one of those wire coils with a sliding contact on top.
Later on, of course, we had a regular radio.
But that was still early in Vienna.
With the lamps, the-- the tube radio.
And the newspapers in Vienna were very well-informed.
So it-- we knew that Hindenburg, being a senile old guy, couldn't do it anymore.
And he appointed Adolph Hitler as chancellor.
In that-- in that respect that in our apartment house, in a sub level room was a storage and a small store.
